params.text:
A no-blame, learning culture

The purpose of reporting is to learn, not to punish. In a just, no-blame culture, staff feel safe to report honestly, including their own mistakes. When something goes wrong, the questions to ask are “What happened and why?” and “How do we stop it happening again?” — not “Whose fault is it?”

Good services investigate events to understand the underlying causes and then make changes — new training, better equipment, or clearer procedures — to prevent recurrence.

Keeping records confidential

Accident and incident records often contain personal and health information. Under data protection law (UK GDPR) this information must be:

  • Kept secure — stored so that only authorised people can access it.
  • Shared only on a need-to-know basis with those who have a legitimate reason.
  • Accurate, relevant and kept no longer than necessary.

Being confidential does not mean hiding what happened from the people affected — the duty of candour still applies. It means handling the records responsibly and respecting people’s privacy.

params.question: Which statement best reflects a healthy reporting culture in adult social care?

answers.text: Staff report events openly, including their own errors, and the service investigates to learn and prevent recurrence.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Openness plus learning is the heart of a just, no-blame culture that keeps people safer.

answers.text: Staff only report events they are certain will not get them into trouble.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Fear of blame hides risks. A just culture encourages honest reporting so lessons are learned.

answers.text: Records are shared freely with anyone to show how transparent the service is.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Openness with those affected is right, but records still contain personal data and must be kept confidential and secure.

answers.text: The main aim of an investigation is to identify who to discipline.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. The aim is to understand causes and prevent recurrence, not to find someone to blame.
